title: The 5-Day SSB Interview Procedure: A Technical Breakdown of Selection Parameters
author: Defense Assessment Desk
data: An objective, documentation-based analysis of the Service Selection Board testing methodology, detailing Phase I Screening, Phase II Psychometric Evaluative Batteries, GTO Obstacle Specifications, and Medical Standards.
excerpt: A professional analysis of the 5-Day SSB Interview protocol. Examine the precise testing mechanisms of the OIR, PP&DT, TAT, WAT, SRT, and GTO series utilized by the Indian Armed Forces to measure Officer Like Qualities.

content.text: The Service Selection Board (SSB) interview protocol is a specialized psychometric and behavioral assessment matrix executed over five operational days. Utilized by the Indian Army, Indian Navy, Indian Air Force, and Indian Coast Guard, the system evaluates candidates who have cleared written national competitive benchmarks like the NDA Exam, UPSC CDS Exam, or AFCAT. The evaluation system is fundamentally structured around a triad of methodologies: Mansa (Psychological appraisal), Vacha (Interview assessment), and Karmana (Practical field evaluation). Rather than measuring academic knowledge, the board assesses candidates against a standardized framework of 15 distinct Officer Like Qualities (OLQs) to determine their viability for a military commission.
SSB Access Channels and Documentation Profile

Aspirants route into the SSB pipeline either through institutional written examinations or designated open entries. These open and specialized frameworks encompass the Technical Graduate Course (TGC), Technical Entry Scheme (TES), Short Service Commission Technology (SSC Tech), Judge Advocate General (JAG) branch, NCC Special Entry Scheme, University Entry Scheme (UES), and specialized non-technical or branch-specific paths such as the Pilot/Observer and Education branches.

Upon qualifying, the respective service headquarters issues the official SSB Call Letter. This administrative directive mandates operational conditions including the applicant's unique identification parameters (Roll Number and Registration Number), the assigned Selection Centre (e.g., Selection Centre East Allahabad, Selection Centre Central Bhopal, Selection Centre South Bangalore, or Selection Centre North Kapurthala), exact logistical arrival vectors, and specific mandatory documentation portfolios including bank details for Travel Allowance (TA) processing for fresh candidates.
The Two-Phase Operational Framework

The testing architecture is divided into two distinct, sequential phases. Phase I serves as an initial filtering mechanism, while Phase II provides an in-depth behavioral profile spanning four consecutive days.
Day 0: Administrative Reception and Reporting

Candidates report directly to the Selection Centre or coordinate via the local military Movement Control Office (MCO) stationed at the primary rail junction. The reception routine enforces primary verification of academic certificates, biometric enrollment, and the assignment of a temporary chest number. This chest number functions as the candidate's sole objective identifier throughout the Phase I processing window to eliminate structural bias.
Day 1: Phase I Screening Protocol
The Screening Stage acts as a high-throughput filter, assessing baseline cognitive functions and basic visual-narrative capabilities. It comprises two primary components:

content: Officer Intelligence Rating (OIR) Test: A dual-booklet examination containing a combined series of approximately 50 verbal and non-verbal reasoning problems. Candidates are bound to a strict 30-minute execution window per booklet, measuring spatial awareness, logical sequence identification, and rapid analytical processing.

Picture Perception & Discussion Test (PP&DT): A multi-tiered projective test. A structurally ambiguous, hazy image is projected on a screen for exactly 30 seconds. Candidates are granted 1 minute to record basic environmental attributes (number of characters, age, sex, and mood markers) and exactly 4 minutes to write a narrative text detailing the context, current action, and ultimate resolution of the scene. Candidates are subsequently segregated into sub-groups (typically 12-15 individuals) arranged in a semi-circle. Each candidate receives 1 minute for individual narrative delivery, immediately followed by an unstructured 10-15 minute group discussion to negotiate a single consolidated group narrative.

content.text: The Phase I framework evaluates core behavioral indices: Power of Expression, Social Cooperation, Self-Confidence, Group Influence, and Pacing of Decisions. Following statistical aggregation, the Phase I results are formally announced. Screened-in individuals undergo secondary document verification, complete detailed Personal Information Questionnaires (PIQ) for Phase II reference, and receive permanent testing chest numbers. Unsuccessful candidates are dismissed following Travel Allowance settlement.
Day 2: Phase II Psychometric Evaluative Battery

Day 2 subjects candidates to a rigorous battery of projective psychological assessments designed to map out the conscious, subconscious, and unconscious operational spaces of the mind without external guidelines or constraints. The testing sequence includes:

content: Thematic Apperception Test (TAT): A series of 12 distinct visual slides presented sequentially. Eleven slides contain ambiguous black-and-white images, while the 12th slide is completely blank. Each image displays for 30 seconds, followed by a fixed 4-minute drafting window where the candidate must formulate a complete story showcasing goal-oriented actions and resolutions.

Word Association Test (WAT): A rapid-fire cognitive reflex test where 60 distinct words are projected sequentially for 15 seconds each. Within those 15 seconds, the candidate must perceive the stimulus word and record their first natural, spontaneous sentence response.

Situation Reaction Test (SRT): A booklet outlining 60 specific real-world crises, interpersonal challenges, or emergency scenarios. Candidates are given 30 minutes to record their practical, physical actions for all 60 scenarios, testing practical reasoning and operational safety logic.

Self-Description Test (SD): A structured context where candidates write dedicated paragraphs detailing the objective opinions their parents, educators/employers, peers, and they themselves hold regarding their personal attributes, explicitly defining their core strengths and actionable development areas.

content.text: Days 3 & 4: Phase II Group Testing Officer (GTO) Series

The GTO series transfers the evaluation matrix to outdoor situational challenges, tracking group dynamics, physical resource coordination, and functional leadership capabilities under stress. The series is executed across nine specific tasks divided between two days:

content: Group Discussion (GD): Two consecutive, open-ended discussions lasting 15-20 minutes each on socio-economic, geopolitical, or contemporary defense issues.

Group Planning Exercise (GPE): A spatial map-based scenario detailing a series of concurrent, time-sensitive physical emergencies. Candidates independently analyze parameters and write solutions within 10 minutes, followed by a 20-minute group reconciliation phase to present a unified plan on a 2D/3D model.

Progressive Group Task (PGT): A multi-stage outdoor obstacle course spanning four levels of increasing technical difficulty. The entire group must navigate across out-of-bounds terrain with a heavy load using only auxiliary bridging tools: a wooden plank (fatta), a structural pole (bali), and a rope under strict design constraints.
Half Group Task (HGT): A direct structural adaptation of the PGT where the unit is halved by the GTO to increase the visibility of individual contributions and technical mechanics.

Group Obstacle Race (Command Race): A highly physical, competitive race against external groups where the entire team must cooperatively carry a rolled canvas structure through physical barriers like high walls, spider webs, and trenches.

Lecturette: A spontaneous public presentation test. The candidate selects 1 out of 4 topics from an index card, receives 3 minutes for analytical preparation, and delivers a structured 3-minute brief to the group.

Individual Obstacle Task (IOT): A test of pure physical agility, coordination, and speed. Candidates work alone to navigate 10 distinct obstacles (including the Burma Bridge, Tarzan Swing, Tiger Leap, and Zig-Zag Balance) within a 3-minute limit, with each obstacle assigned a weight from 1 to 10 marks.

Command Task: A leadership assessment where the candidate is designated as a Commander, reviews a unique complex obstacle structure, selects two subordinates as resources, and directs them to execute a tactical bypass.
Final Group Task (FGT): A single-stage group obstacle task designed to confirm final observations regarding team alignment and collective operational pacing.

content.text: Operational Note on Personal Interviews: Independent of the GTO routine, candidates are sequentially summoned for a comprehensive Personal Interview by the Interviewing Officer (IO) between Days 2 and 4. Lasting from 45 to 90 minutes, this evaluation utilizes the PIQ data to thoroughly examine the candidate's core motivations, domestic background, emotional intelligence, and real-time situational awareness.
Day 5: The Board Conference

The final day brings the entire testing panel—including the President, Deputy President, Interviewing Officers, GTOs, and Psychologists—together in full uniform to formalize recommendations. Each candidate enters the boardroom individually for a brief review. The panel reviews the independent findings across the three core dimensions. In borderline cases, the panel may introduce specialized situation-based questions to verify behavioral consistency before finalizing the recommendation consensus.
Computerized Pilot Selection System (CPSS) Specifications

Candidates vying for the Flying Branches of the Indian Armed Forces undergo the Computerized Pilot Selection System (CPSS), a highly specialized, once-in-a-lifetime test. Executed post-recommendation or after Day 4, the system measures visual-spatial coordination, psychomotor tracking speed, and auditory response times through the Instrument Battery Test, Sensory Motor Apparatus Test (SMAT), and Control Velocity Test (CVT).
Medical Board Classification & Disqualifications

Recommended candidates report directly to the Special Medical Board (SMB) for a comprehensive clinical diagnostic review. Candidates failing basic physical indices face either Permanent Medical Rejection or Temporary Medical Unfitness. Common reasons for permanent disqualification include structural anomalies like Flat Foot, Knock Knees, Pigeon Chest, severe Carrying Angles of the elbow, persistent Sweaty Palms, and inadequate Color Perception baselines (CP2/CP3 depending on entry path). Candidates with correctable variances (e.g., body mass index issues) are granted a 42-day window to apply to the Appeal Medical Board (AMB) with a nominal processing fee at regional Command Hospitals.
The Evaluative Metric: The 15 Officer Like Qualities (OLQs)
All testing exercises track performance variations across 4 factor clusters, which comprise the 15 official Officer Like Qualities (OLQs) mapped by the Indian Armed Forces:

content: Factor 1: Planning and Organizing — Effective Intelligence (applied problem-solving), Reasoning Ability (logical analysis), Organizing Ability (resource structuring), and Power of Expression (lucid communication).
Factor 2: Social Adjustment — Social Adaptability (interpersonal blending), Cooperation (collective execution), and Sense of Responsibility (accountability and dependability).

Factor 3: Social Effectiveness — Initiative (proactive leadership), Self-Confidence (composure under stress), Speed of Decision (timely prioritization), Ability to Influence the Group (logical persuasion), and Liveliness (resilient optimism).
Factor 4: Dynamic Factor — Determination (persistence), Courage (risk management), and physical/mental Stamina (endurance).
